growth_records.medium: Mineral medium + 20 mM HEPES + 3 mM nitrate (CO/NO3- activity assay, no antibiotics)

up.method: reported CO oxidation rate (14.3% vol/vol CO, nitrate acceptor)

growth_records.cite: Egas et al. Carbon monoxide metabolism in freshwater anaerobic methanotrophic archaea. Nature Communications (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41467-026-70080-4. [PMC13079737]
growth_records.snippet: Under nitrate respiratory conditions, the culture oxidized CO (14.3% vol/vol in the headspace) at high rates that reached 475 µmol d-1 gDW-1.
growth_records.notes: Batch activity assay (no growth rate; authors state no growth-yield conclusions drawn). CO oxidation (uptake) = 475 µmol/d/gDW = 0.475 mmol/d/gDW ÷ 24 = 0.0198 mmol/gDW/h. Coupled to nitrate reduction, product CO2. Fig 1A (n=2).

growth_records.medium: Mineral medium + 20 mM HEPES + 3 mM nitrate (CH4/NO3- activity assay, no antibiotics)

up.method: reported CH4 oxidation rate (no CO, nitrate acceptor)

growth_records.cite: Egas et al. Carbon monoxide metabolism in freshwater anaerobic methanotrophic archaea. Nature Communications (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41467-026-70080-4. [PMC13079737]
growth_records.snippet: the methane oxidation rate (in the absence of CO) proceeded more slowly at 210 µmol d-1 gDW-1 (Fig. 1A).
growth_records.notes: CH4 oxidation (uptake) = 210 µmol/d/gDW = 0.210 mmol/d/gDW ÷ 24 = 0.00875 mmol/gDW/h, coupled to nitrate reduction; product CO2. Fig 1A (n=2).

growth_records.medium: Mineral medium + 20 mM HEPES + 3 mM nitrate (CO + CH4 co-fed, no antibiotics)

up.method: reported CH4 oxidation rate (7.1% CH4) — strongly inhibited by CO

growth_records.cite: Egas et al. Carbon monoxide metabolism in freshwater anaerobic methanotrophic archaea. Nature Communications (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41467-026-70080-4. [PMC13079737]
growth_records.snippet: When adding volumetric equal amounts of CO and CH4 (7.1% vol/vol each) the CO oxidation rate (952 µmol d-1 gDW-1) far exceeded the CH4 oxidation rate (43 µmol d-1 gDW-1).
growth_records.notes: Co-feeding CO+CH4 (7.1% each): CO uptake 952 µmol/d/gDW (=0.0397 mmol/gDW/h), CH4 uptake 43 µmol/d/gDW (=0.00179 mmol/gDW/h). CO strongly inhibits CH4 oxidation (substrate preference for CO). Fig 1B (n=2).

growth_records.medium: Mineral medium + 20 mM HEPES + 3 mM nitrate + SVAK antibiotics (CO/NO3-)
growth_records.cite: Egas et al. Carbon monoxide metabolism in freshwater anaerobic methanotrophic archaea. Nature Communications (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41467-026-70080-4. [PMC13079737]
growth_records.snippet: During incubations with CO/NO3− (CO oxidation rate 979 µmol d-1 gDW-1), no accumulation of CH4, formate or acetate was detected (Fig. 1C).
growth_records.notes: With bacteria suppressed (SVAK), CO/NO3- CO oxidation = 979 µmol/d/gDW (=0.0408 mmol/gDW/h). No CH4/formate/acetate — electrons routed entirely to respiratory nitrate reduction, CO2 sole product. Fig 1C (n=4).

growth_records.medium: Mineral medium + 20 mM HEPES + 3 mM nitrate + SVAK antibiotics (CH4/NO3-)
growth_records.cite: Egas et al. Carbon monoxide metabolism in freshwater anaerobic methanotrophic archaea. Nature Communications (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41467-026-70080-4. [PMC13079737]
growth_records.snippet: In incubations with CH4/NO3− (CH4 oxidation rate 85 µmol d-1 gDW-1), CO2 was the only oxidation product (Fig. 1C).
growth_records.notes: With SVAK antibiotics, CH4/NO3- CH4 oxidation = 85 µmol/d/gDW (=0.00354 mmol/gDW/h), CO2 sole product. Fig 1C (n=4).

growth_records.medium: Mineral medium + 20 mM HEPES, NO nitrate, CO/CO2 acetogenic + SVAK

sec.method: near-linear accumulation throughout incubation (rate not numeric)
rapid accumulation first 20 h then slower; overflow metabolism

growth_records.cite: Egas et al. Carbon monoxide metabolism in freshwater anaerobic methanotrophic archaea. Nature Communications (2026). DOI:10.1038/s41467-026-70080-4. [PMC13079737]
growth_records.snippet: CO oxidation rates (at 7.1% vol/vol) were even higher than under nitrate respiratory conditions and reached 1261 µmol d-1 gDW-1 ... CH4 was produced at a rate ... (99 µmol d-1 gDW-1) ... acetate was produced with a near-linear rate.
growth_records.notes: Nitrate-free CO/CO2: CO oxidation (uptake) = 1261 µmol/d/gDW (=0.0525 mmol/gDW/h), the highest of all conditions. Products: CH4 99 µmol/d/gDW (=0.004125 mmol/gDW/h), plus acetate (near-linear) and formate (overflow) — specific rates for acetate/formate not given numerically. Carbon balance closed (Suppl Data 4). Fig 1D (n=3).
